finding: Negative perception of a social group's impact on national community reduces trust in political institutions and elites in democracies; this relationship is stronger in countries with longer immigration history. Contextual factors mediate individual-level effects of group threat on institutional confidence.

Regular practice switching between two linguistic systems enhances flexible reconfiguration of stimulus-response associations in non-linguistic task switching. The global switch cost (task-set interference resolution) is smaller for bilinguals than monolinguals when response incompatibility is high.

Observers estimate longer prior sequences when witnessing rare or unusual outcomes than when observing common outcomes from identical random processes. Perceived rarity (rather than objective probability) drives retrospective inference about sequence length.

Priming anticipated regret and self-directed justification reduces the decoy effect and eliminates preference for dominated-but-attractive options. Accountability to external audiences maintains or exacerbates decoy effects; internal self-scrutiny undermines them.

Providing an alternative causal explanation for an outcome is significantly more effective than denial or counter-evidence at preventing acceptance of false causal inferences. Causal correction displaces initial causal links that people formed between false claims and outcomes.

Activating gender stereotypes during a political knowledge test reduces female performance and benefits male performance, consistent with stereotype threat and lift mechanisms. Situational stereotype salience explains variation in the gender gap independent of stable individual or socialization factors.
